verses.text: 19:1–21:25 Ce second épilogue (voir note sur **17.1–21.25**) déplace l'attention des individus aux tribus entières. Commençant par l'outrage dans la cité benjaminite de Guibea (**ch. 19**), qui mena à la guerre civile (**ch. 20**) et à la décimation de Benjamin, puis à la tentative de sauver la tribu de la disparition (**ch. 21**), le livre se conclut sur le refrain désormais familier : *En ces jours-là, il n'y avait pas de roi en Israël ; chacun faisait ce qui lui semblait bon*. C'était peut-être le point le plus bas de la mémoire historique d'Israël ; *ce qu'ils firent à Guibea* qu'Osée, quatre cents ans plus tard, comparerait à la profondeur où Éphraïm était tombé (**Os 9.9 ; 10.9**). • La voie était ouverte pour une transition d'un leadership charismatique à ce qu'on pourrait appeler une « royauté covenanto-charismatique » : une royauté combinant la vie sous l'alliance du Seigneur (**Dt 17.14-20**) et l'onction de l'Esprit pour la tâche de gouverner, comme avec Saül et David. 19:1 Une **concubine** était une épouse de second rang. Dans une société où la polygamie était courante, une concubine pouvait être achetée, acquise en remboursement de dette ou prise à la guerre. Une concubine venait parfois compenser une carence chez une épouse légitime (p. ex. **Gn 16.1-4 ; 30.3-13 ; 35.22**), mais dans le cas du Lévite il ne semble pas y avoir eu d'autre épouse.

19:4 Le père de la jeune femme manifeste l'hospitalité typique du Proche-Orient, contrastant avec ce qui surviendrait à Guibea (**19.14-28**). La scène à Bethléhem offre réconciliation et promesse d'un nouveau commencement.

19:5-7 Bien que certains aient suggéré que le retard à partir évoque le festin nuptial de sept jours de Samson (**14.10-20**), l'image ne reflète peut-être rien de plus que la réticence du père à dire adieu à sa fille.

19:5-7 Bien que certains aient suggéré que le retard à partir évoque le festin nuptial de sept jours de Samson (**14.10-20**), l'image ne reflète peut-être rien de plus que la réticence du père à dire adieu à sa fille.

19:5-7 Bien que certains aient suggéré que le retard à partir évoque le festin nuptial de sept jours de Samson (**14.10-20**), l'image ne reflète peut-être rien de plus que la réticence du père à dire adieu à sa fille.

19:8 tarried—with reluctance. until afternoon—literally, "the decline of the day." People in the East, who take little or nothing to eat in the morning, do not breakfast till from ten to twelve A.M., and this meal the hospitable relative had purposely protracted to so late a period as to afford an argument for urging a further stay.

19:9 the day draweth toward evening—Hebrew, "the pitching time of day." Travellers who set out at daybreak usually halt about the middle of the afternoon the first day, to enjoy rest and refreshment. It was, then, too late a time to commence a journey. But duty, perhaps, obliged the Levite to indulge no further delay.
19:10 **Yebous** : voir note sur **1.21**. L'ancien nom de la cité et son statut étranger en Israël persistèrent jusqu'à sa prise par David (**2S 5.6-10**).

19:11 And when they were by Jebus the day was far spent,.... Or "was gone down very much" (q), the sun was going down apace, and near setting: and the servant said unto his master, come, I pray thee: he proposed it to him in a submissive manner, and might use some entreaty for his master's good and safety: and let us turn in unto this city of the Jebusites, and lodge in it; for though that part of the city which belonged to the tribe of Judah was taken by them after the death of Joshua, yet that which belonged to the tribe of Benjamin, part of it was still possessed by the Jebusites, whom the Benjamites could not expel, Jdg 1:21 and Ben Gersom thinks, that this affair of…

19:12 And his master said unto him, we will not turn aside hither into the city of a stranger,.... A city of the children of the Gentiles, as the Targum, as the Jebusites were, being one of the seven nations of the land of Canaan, who were to be dispossessed and destroyed; and which, one would think, he would not have so called, had it been inhabited, though but in part, by the children of Judah and Benjamin: by this it appears, that this Levite was a good man, and conscientious, and shunned all appearance of evil, and the company of wicked men, as became his character and profession: and it is added: that is not of the children of Israel;

19:13 Les deux cités benjaminites de **Guibea** et **Rama** occupent une place importante dans l'histoire d'Israël, liées qu'elles sont aux juges (Déborah, **4.5**), aux prêtres (Éléazar, **Jos 24.33**), aux prophètes (Samuel, **1S 8.4**) et aux rois (Saül, **1S 10.26**). Le récit de l'histoire noble de Guibea aggrave la tragédie des événements qui suivirent.

19:14 And they passed on, and went their way,.... Proceeded on in their journey from Jebus or Jerusalem, near to which they were: and the sun went down upon them when they were by Gibeah, which belongeth to Benjamin; which is added, to distinguish it from another Gibeah in the tribe of Judah, Jos 15:57 when they were come pretty near to this place, on the side of it, as it seems, the sun was just setting, which determined them to take up their lodging here, as follows.

19:15 Les règles habituelles d'hospitalité en Israël et dans le reste du Proche-Orient obligeaient un citoyen de la ville à les recueillir (cp. **Es 58.7 ; Lc 14.13-14 ; Rm 12.13 ; 1Tm 3.2 ; 5.10 ; Hé 13.2 ; 1P 4.9**). Le Lévite avait des fonds et des provisions (**Jg 19.19**) ; l'excuse était donc encore plus mince. Cette érosion de la civilité ordinaire est une autre preuve du dysfonctionnement social du temps des Juges.
19:16 Le vieillard venait de la **région montagneuse d'Éphraïm**, et non du territoire de Benjamin. Les autochtones de Guibea, tous, refusèrent leur aide.

19:17 And when he had lifted up his eyes,.... For it may be, as he came out of the field, he was musing and meditating with his eyes downwards directed, but coming into the city looked up: he saw a wayfaring man in the street of the city; whom he supposed to be a traveller and a stranger by his dress, and other circumstances, having never seen him before, and knowing pretty well the inhabitants of the place: and the old man said, whither goest thou?
19:18 *Maintenant je rentre chez moi* : le Lévite ne dit rien de son propre statut ni de la raison de son voyage.

19:19 there is no want of any thing—In answering the kindly inquiries of the old man, the Levite deemed it right to state that he was under no necessity of being burdensome on anyone, for he possessed all that was required to relieve his wants. Oriental travellers always carry a stock of provisions with them; and knowing that even the khans or lodging-houses they may find on their way afford nothing beyond rest and shelter, they are careful to lay in a supply of food both for themselves and their beasts.
19:20 L'urgence dans la réponse du vieillard préfigure le trouble à venir (cp. **Gn 19.2-3**).

19:21 So he brought him into his house,.... Showed him the way to it, and in a kind and friendly manner introduced him, and bid him welcome: and gave provender unto the asses; this is mentioned first, it being then perhaps, as now, the first thing that a careful man is concerned for, to see that his cattle is taken care of, and then himself; and such a method this ancient good man took with his guest: and they washed their feet;
19:22 **Fauteurs de troubles** : hébreu *fils de Bélial* ; voir notes sur **1R 21.10 ; 1S 1.16 ; 2Co 6.15**. Leur but était le viol homosexuel. Cp. **Gn 19.4-5**.

19:23 *Car cet homme est un hôte dans ma maison* : il était honteux de manquer d'hospitalité envers un visiteur, mais les exigences malveillantes de la foule allaient bien au-delà du défaut d'hospitalité. Ils voulaient violenter le visiteur (voir **20.5**).

19:24 Le vieillard tente de préserver l'obligation sociale d'hospitalité au prix de livrer les vulnérables à la violence (cp. **Jc 1.27**). Rien, pour notre époque comme pour la leur, ne peut adoucir l'horreur de ce qui suivit. La période des juges touchait à sa fin dans la plus profonde dépravation. Quelque chose était fondamentalement détraqué.
19:25 Contraster l'instruction aux maris en **Ep 5.25-28**.

19:26 Then came the woman in the dawning of the day,.... When those wicked wretches who had abused her had left her, having had her from place to place, at some distance from the house out of which she was put, and to which she got again, as well as she could: and fell down at the door of the man's house where her lord was; her husband, so called, not because she had been his servant, but because she was his wife;

19:27 And her lord rose up in the morning,.... Very early no doubt, and it is scarcely reasonable to think he should sleep quietly after such a riot, and his concubine or wife delivered up to the lust of such brutish creatures: and opened the doors of the house, and went out to go his way; either in search of her, or rather to make the best of his way on his journey, to preserve his own life, having given her up for lost: and, behold the woman his concubine was fallen down at the door of the house, and her hands were upon the threshold;
19:28-29 Les actes du Lévite illustrent l'horreur d'une culture corrompue.
19:28-29 Les actes du Lévite illustrent l'horreur d'une culture corrompue.

19:30 And it was so, that all that saw it,.... The dismembered pieces of the dead body, and were made acquainted with the cause of it; for so Josephus (b) says, he gave the messengers a command to declare what was the cause of her death: said, there was no such deed done nor seen, from the day that the children of Israel came out of Egypt, unto this day; meaning not so much the cutting in pieces the dead body, and sending it to different parts;
